`proxy_url` requires information from the route to work correctly. It can't just take the path from the request and mash it onto the upstream base url. It needs to know what subset of the request path to mash onto the url (which is typically achieved with a named pattern).

In your test, `proxy_url` will always equal the base url unless you specific a `url` kwarg to the view. The url `kwarg` is appended to the `base_url`

When a consumer is running that doesn't currently have anything to do it will print out a constant stream of when poll is called in a loop (which I assume is the normal method of consuming endlessly):
"_poll called with a non-zero timeout and sleep=False -- but there was nothing to do. This can cause high CPU usage during idle."

This is similar to issue #494 that had been fixed but it seems that commit b8c2097 broke it again. I feel that it is unneeded work for every client to check on the partition assignments before every call to poll to make sure that it is non-empty. For the client, there is little difference between receiving no data because there are no partitions currently assigned and receiving no data because the topic has no new messages. So allowing poll to sleep if a timeout is specified helps simplying the usage of the library.

This method minimizes the number of times that `gpmdp-remote` needs to be run, as well as provides a simple 1:1 mapping between format string parameters and the output of gpmdp-remote. It's efficient (in LOC, but it's also not bad in terms of runtime), and easy to use/understand (don't know if  I would say the same for the common `format.format()` convention I keep seeing -- at least name the _variable_/_member_ `format` something like `format_str` to differentiate it from the _method_ `format`? That being said, I'm _not_ saying that I think this GPMDP method is suitable for every module...). 

The alternative dictionary method would be to parse out the artist, album, track, current time, and total track length every time the module is called. That requires 4-5 runs of gpmdp-remote, as well as a bunch of extra code. With this method, if someone wants just the default output ('info'), gpmdp-remote only gets run once. 'artist' and 'track'? Yes, I didn't want to change the current default behavior. But using the current regex I couldn't take any number because it filters them. 

Therefore I've just add a kind of filter in the regex: if the token doesn't match using the first part (the current `\w[\w']`), it tries with the second one (`\w+`). You can test this regex with samples of text if you're not convinced, for instance in this link: https://regex101.com/. But you can see that it's just a modification to let numbers be matched (and they will be removed in the snippet https://github.com/leferrad/word_cloud/blob/number_rendering_support/wordcloud/wordcloud.py#L519-L521 if the argument `render_numbers=False`), but it doesn't cancel the previous regex behavior I guess. 

I'm agree with the fact that it's not fine to "switch" a regex based on the parameter `render_numbers` because it could fork the behavior, so that's the reason why I've just override the current regex. However, if you still uncomfortable with that override, and I can look for another way to achieve this intended feature.

When we have a packet with an unknown or unsupported body type, this allows us to return the valid parsed header data, rather than having an all-or-nothing parsing.  This is particularly important for third-party packets, where the third-party packet is well-formed but the original one is either malformed or unsupported.

For a project I'm working on, I'm interested as much in the headers as in the data, so this allows me to use packets where the path is well-known but the message is malformed or unsupported.  It seems more efficient to return the partial decode than to return an error and have to then catch it and parse just the header again.

Updating an existing `<head>` favicon via native JS can be as short as:

```js
document.getElementById('favicon').href = '<new URL>';
```

Given the above, and that we don't use any of [react-favicon](https://github.com/oflisback/react-favicon)'s more advanced features (animations, dynamic bubbles, ...) how do you think the native JS approach might compare?

The `failure` field is assigned when PyMongo receives a remote error, in format of BSON. PyMongo decodes it into a python `dict` and wrap into an `OperationError`.

try:

@linkerzhang I don't quite understand which copy are you trying to avoid:

There are four phases:
1. caller pass ModelProto to optimizer
2. optimizer converts ModelProto to Graph
3. optimizer passes Graph to each pass
4. optimizer converts Graph back to ModelProto
5. optimizer returns the new ModelProto

1 won't be doing copy anymore after this change, 3 is doing in-place modification already so no copy, 2 and 4 are by design doing copy so passes writer can more easily manipulate the underlying data (and to be fair not very related to this PR). 5 doesn't have copy thanks to RVO (and thanks to @yinghai pointed this out then leads to this PR).
